query: I'm building a small desktop timer app in SwiftUI. It already follows the system light/dark appearance automatically, so theming is covered, right? I'd rather not clutter the settings screen with an appearance option nobody changes.
expected_behavior: States that automatic system-following alone does not satisfy the theming rule: the app must also ship a persisted, user-facing THREE-STATE appearance control — System / Light / Dark — defaulting to System, wherever the app has any settings surface
Rejects a binary light/dark toggle as insufficient because it loses the 'follow the system' state
Stores the choice as a per-device preference (UserDefaults / localStorage), explicitly NOT in synced or server-side app state — the same user may want different looks per device
Binds the control to the platform mechanism (macOS NSApp.appearance / SwiftUI preferredColorScheme; web data-theme + prefers-color-scheme fallback), applied at launch before first render
anti_behavior: Accepts 'it follows the system automatically' as sufficient theming
Proposes a two-state light/dark toggle with no way back to following the system
Puts the appearance choice into synced app state or a server-side profile
Treats the appearance control as optional clutter to be skipped because few users change it
source: 2026-07-20 Rosso (Pomodoro app): light/dark support existed and followed the system, but the human had to explicitly request the System/Light/Dark override as a feature — the skill was silent on the user-facing control, its three-state shape, and its per-device (never-synced) storage.
